# Source Card: Laboratory diagnosis of G6PD deficiency. A British Society for Haematology Guideline

## Summary
This 2020 British Society for Haematology practice guideline (an update of the 1991 General Haematology Task Force guideline) sets the laboratory standard for diagnosing G6PD deficiency: enzyme activity must be measured directly by a quantitative spectrophotometric assay, not by screening tests alone (especially in females). It establishes that reticulocytosis during or soon after acute hemolysis (and recent transfusion) can produce a false-normal result, and recommends re-assay at least 2-3 months after a hemolytic episode when suspicion persists. For the pediatric CDS module it also documents that neonatal/infant and premature-infant reference values deviate markedly from adult values.

## Key evidence (with exact-passage locators)
- (ev_001) Diagnose by direct quantitative spectrophotometric assay; a quantitative assay must follow any abnormal/borderline screen — locator: Page 5, Recommendations: Laboratory diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:5e564d319cadfd6a8af990714bdefe8dd2593fb845f5e741aba8a95866ab157a]"
- (ev_002) Assay during/soon after hemolysis can be false-normal because reticulocytes carry higher G6PD activity — locator: Page 6, Factors affecting clinical interpretation — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:34717637d157b3d7b45f49516da950ce75ac5dd579fcf2f0de3b31e48e9868ee]"
- (ev_003) Repeat the assay at least 2-3 months after the hemolytic episode when suspicion remains — locator: Page 6, Factors affecting clinical interpretation (+ Page 5 recommendation) — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:80c3b56e8876f3ae441790ebd7ef6509ba0ff7e76b19985712135441a3528c77]"
- (ev_004) Recent transfusion (or reticulocytosis) can mask deficiency; consider molecular analysis — locator: Page 7, Molecular diagnosis — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:de81d7ab38ded7cd2e36396dffc2dcc4c7682f9a968ba2ec24e4dd75e716f1bf]"
- (ev_005) WHO/variant classification by residual activity as % of normal (Class I/II <10%, III 10-60%, IV 100%, V >100%) — locator: Page 3, Table I — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:d21a866f2e2fc88c766d5df7d37df6b8340a6cb71647810232475de0e035ba40]"
- (ev_006) Adult normal reference values in iu/g Hb at 30 C and 37 C — locator: Page 6, Normal values — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:4a693be9a45db3a32ddbcf9b2d5d9fd6b96bf85249e9f911dbf3d7a9742cebaf]"
- (ev_007) Newborn/infant and premature values deviate from adult (newborn mean ~150% of adult) — locator: Page 6, Normal values — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:d88dd8667d7c5058ce4b5b354368e98f26e912d8a4605be290f6ec5dbc10cb76]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Assay is method- and temperature-dependent: WHO one-substrate assay vs Glock & McLean (two-substrate) method; run at 30 C or 37 C; results expressed as iu/g haemoglobin or per 10^12 RBC. Each laboratory must establish its own reference range (ICSH in-house or commercial kit) — the kit manufacturer's range is "too broad and will provide a false-normal result on many heterozygotes" (Page 5-6).
- Population dependence is pediatric-critical: adult reference values (ev_006) do NOT apply to neonates/infants/premature infants (ev_007); interpretation must also account for reticulocyte count and low MCH (thalassemia/iron deficiency overestimates activity per g Hb).
- Guideline version: BSH 2020 (Br J Haematol 2020;189(1):24-38), effective 2020-01, updates the 1991 guideline; reviewed on a 3-year cycle. GRADE-graded recommendations (mostly GRADE 1C).

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- Table III ("Indications for G6PD testing", Page 3) defines when to test — e.g., haemolytic anaemia in the newborn (non-immune), prolonged or severe neonatal jaundice, favism, red-cell morphology suggestive of oxidant damage or positive Heinz body stain, congenital non-spherocytic haemolytic anaemia. These function as testing/referral triggers relevant to the CDS module. Prolonged or severe neonatal jaundice is an explicit newborn-specific testing indication.

## Limitations & conflicts
- No numeric diagnostic cutoff for "deficient" is expressed as a single verbatim activity value in this guideline — the guideline deliberately directs each lab to establish its own in-house reference range rather than publishing a universal cutoff. GAP: a specific "deficient if < X% or < X iu/g Hb" clinical cutoff is NOT locatable in BSH2020_G6PD (the 30%/70% treatment thresholds cited in secondary summaries were NOT found verbatim in this guideline text; do not attribute them to this source). Class-based % ranges (ev_005) and adult reference means (ev_006) are the only verbatim numeric anchors.
- The KB record's URL is a PubMed search URL, not the article; corrected canonical locators recorded here (DOI 10.1111/bjh.16366, PMID 31991476).
- Full text was read from a mirrored offprint PDF; page numbers refer to the offprint (pages 2-8), which correspond to journal pages 25-31 of the 24-38 article range.
- No direct conflict with other anemia-KB sources recorded; this source is co-cited with BLOOD2022_PED_ANEMIA on transfusion-interpretation notes and complements rather than contradicts it.
